topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt too harsh, bleaching the vibrant hues of my canvas into something sterile and unwelcome. I stood by the entrance, my knuckles white where I gripped my worn tote bag. Every patron who paused before my piece, their murmurs of appreciation directed at the swirling blues and deep crimsons, felt like a tiny, irritating prickle under my skin. "Excuse me," I managed, my voice tight, forcing a smile that felt brittle on my face. "This is... mine." A woman, draped in silk, glanced at the painting, then back at me with an expression that suggested she hadn't quite grasped the gravity of the situation. I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to keep the tremor out of my hands as I navigated the crowd, the sheer audacity of the display making my jaw clench.

[story 2]

I watched from across the room as people circled the sculpture, their heads tilted in contemplation. It was my piece, 'Echoes of the City,' sitting there, bathed in the spotlight, completely divorced from my intention. A low thrum of frustration began vibrating in my chest, a restless energy I couldn't quite settle. I walked over, my steps measured and stiff, stopping directly in front of a critic who was gesturing emphatically toward the work. "I must insist," I stated, my tone carefully modulated to sound professional rather than anything less, "that this piece is currently on loan, and I would appreciate it if we could discuss its placement." The critic merely smiled, a slow, patronizing curve of his lips, and waved a dismissive hand, as if my very existence was an inconvenience to his evening.

[story 3]

The silence in the studio felt deafening, amplified by the sounds of polite, yet utterly dismissive, conversation drifting from the viewing area. I paced the narrow space between my easel and the doorway, my movements sharp and jerky. My gaze kept flicking toward the gallery where my work hung, feeling a hot flush rise up my neck. Why hadn't they even bothered to check? It was like watching someone deliberately dismantle a carefully constructed arrangement, piece by piece, without acknowledging the effort involved. I stopped abruptly, running a hand through my hair, the gesture tight and agitated. "This is inappropriate," I said, the words coming out clipped and low, laced with a sharp edge that I immediately regretted, but couldn't quite pull back.
